pages.text:
COMMENTS ON THE SAUDI ELECTRICITY COMPANY MATERIALS STANDARD SPECIFICATION
No. 15-TMSS-07 Revision 0
INTRODUCTION
This document has been prepared in response to the invita(cid:415)on of the 5th January 2016 from the Saudi Electricity
Company to comment on their Transmission Materials Standard Specifica(cid:415)on No. 15-TMSS-07. Being given the
opportunity to do so is much appreciated.
Roy Macey, the author of this report, received his BSc(Eng) degree in 1972 and MSc(Eng) degree in 1981. He is a regis-
tered Professional Engineer, a Fellow of the South African Ins(cid:415)tute of Electrical Engineers, a Member of the Ins(cid:415)tute
of Electrical and Electronic Engineers (USA) and a Dis(cid:415)nguished Member of Cigre.
Mr Macey commenced his studies of insulator pollu(cid:415)on at the University of Cape Town in 1971 with his undergradu-
ate thesis on the subject. He con(cid:415)nued his insulator inves(cid:415)ga(cid:415)ons for a further four years with the na(cid:415)onal u(cid:415)lity,
Eskom’s, Research Division. This was followed by twelve years as the specialist on overhead lines and insulators with
the Cullinan Group. During this (cid:415)me, he designed all of the porcelain insulators manufactured in South Africa. In 1990
he founded Mace Electrical Technologies who, amongst other high voltage ac(cid:415)vi(cid:415)es, developed the CharCoat IC
silicone rubber insulator coa(cid:415)ng and the CharCoat IC insulator upgrading system.
He has delivered numerous papers around the world and presented courses on high voltage insulators in South
Africa, Namibia, New Zealand, Australia, Norway and the United States. He is co-author of the book “The Prac(cid:415)cal
Guide to Outdoor High Voltage Insulators”. His paper "The performance of high voltage outdoor insula(cid:415)on in contam-
inated environments", presented to the SAIEE in 1980, gained a South African Transport Services Award. In 1987, he
was awarded the Telemecanique Electrical Design Award and a Design Ins(cid:415)tute/Shell Design Award for his 22kV over-
head line insula(cid:415)on system.
The comments provided in this document are based on the tes(cid:415)ng and overall service experience gained over the last
thirty five years with the CharCoat IC silicone rubber coa(cid:415)ng material. The performance of CharCoat IC in some of the
most severely polluted environments in the world is well proven. Tens of thousands of litres have been applied on
line and substa(cid:415)on insula(cid:415)on of system voltages up to 765kVAC and 533kVDC. However, being of a more advanced
formula(cid:415)on than other insulator coa(cid:415)ngs on the market, its characteris(cid:415)cs deviate from several clauses in the SEC
specifica(cid:415)on. The difference in the proper(cid:415)es and the resultant effects on field performance are described below.
DESIGN AND MATERIAL REQUIREMENTS
Clause 4.3.1, Table 01: RTV Coa(cid:415)ng Proper(cid:415)es as Supplied.
• “Type – One-part, RTV”
CharCoat Passive Fire Protec(cid:415)on Inc selected a two-part RTV silicone rubber for the CharCoat IC product. This means
that a pre-measured small bo(cid:425)le of Catalyst is supplied with each container of CharCoat IC. The catalyst is poured
into the 1 litre or 5 litre can of coa(cid:415)ng which is then shaken prior to use. (As the SEC Standard for the applica(cid:415)on of
insulator coa(cid:415)ng, TCS-P-122.22, Clause 8.1.2(b), states that the “coa(cid:415)ng shall be mixed thoroughly to ensure even
dispersion before spraying”, the mixing in of the catalyst does not involve any addi(cid:415)onal ac(cid:415)vity.) This is a quick and
simple opera(cid:415)on and offers the following advantages:
o The curing of the rubber is more controlled and not dictated by the amount of moisture in the air.
o Because an outside agent is required for the rubber to cure, the shelf life of the material is considera
bly extended.
Sugges(cid:415)on: Allow both one- and two-part coa(cid:415)ngs.

| COMMENTS ON THE SAUDI ELECTRICITY COMPANY MATERIALS STANDARD SPECIFICATION No. 15-TMSS-07 Revision 0 INTRODUCTION This document has been prepared in response to the invita(cid:415)on of the 5th January 2016 from the Saudi Electricity Company to comment on their Transmission Materials Standard Specifica(cid:415)on No. 15-TMSS-07. Being given the opportunity to do so is much appreciated. Roy Macey, the author of this report, received his BSc(Eng) degree in 1972 and MSc(Eng) degree in 1981. He is a regis- tered Professional Engineer, a Fellow of the South African Ins(cid:415)tute of Electrical Engineers, a Member of the Ins(cid:415)tute of Electrical and Electronic Engineers (USA) and a Dis(cid:415)nguished Member of Cigre. Mr Macey commenced his studies of insulator pollu(cid:415)on at the University of Cape Town in 1971 with his undergradu- ate thesis on the subject. He con(cid:415)nued his insulator inves(cid:415)ga(cid:415)ons for a further four years with the na(cid:415)onal u(cid:415)lity, Eskom’s, Research Division. This was followed by twelve years as the specialist on overhead lines and insulators with the Cullinan Group. During this (cid:415)me, he designed all of the porcelain insulators manufactured in South Africa. In 1990 he founded Mace Electrical Technologies who, amongst other high voltage ac(cid:415)vi(cid:415)es, developed the CharCoat IC silicone rubber insulator coa(cid:415)ng and the CharCoat IC insulator upgrading system. He has delivered numerous papers around the world and presented courses on high voltage insulators in South Africa, Namibia, New Zealand, Australia, Norway and the United States. He is co-author of the book “The Prac(cid:415)cal Guide to Outdoor High Voltage Insulators”. His paper "The performance of high voltage outdoor insula(cid:415)on in contam- inated environments", presented to the SAIEE in 1980, gained a South African Transport Services Award. In 1987, he was awarded the Telemecanique Electrical Design Award and a Design Ins(cid:415)tute/Shell Design Award for his 22kV over- head line insula(cid:415)on system. The comments provided in this document are based on the tes(cid:415)ng and overall service experience gained over the last thirty five years with the CharCoat IC silicone rubber coa(cid:415)ng material. The performance of CharCoat IC in some of the most severely polluted environments in the world is well proven. Tens of thousands of litres have been applied on line and substa(cid:415)on insula(cid:415)on of system voltages up to 765kVAC and 533kVDC. However, being of a more advanced formula(cid:415)on than other insulator coa(cid:415)ngs on the market, its characteris(cid:415)cs deviate from several clauses in the SEC specifica(cid:415)on. The difference in the proper(cid:415)es and the resultant effects on field performance are described below. DESIGN AND MATERIAL REQUIREMENTS Clause 4.3.1, Table 01: RTV Coa(cid:415)ng Proper(cid:415)es as Supplied. • “Type – One-part, RTV” CharCoat Passive Fire Protec(cid:415)on Inc selected a two-part RTV silicone rubber for the CharCoat IC product. This means that a pre-measured small bo(cid:425)le of Catalyst is supplied with each container of CharCoat IC. The catalyst is poured into the 1 litre or 5 litre can of coa(cid:415)ng which is then shaken prior to use. (As the SEC Standard for the applica(cid:415)on of insulator coa(cid:415)ng, TCS-P-122.22, Clause 8.1.2(b), states that the “coa(cid:415)ng shall be mixed thoroughly to ensure even dispersion before spraying”, the mixing in of the catalyst does not involve any addi(cid:415)onal ac(cid:415)vity.) This is a quick and simple opera(cid:415)on and offers the following advantages: o The curing of the rubber is more controlled and not dictated by the amount of moisture in the air. o Because an outside agent is required for the rubber to cure, the shelf life of the material is considera bly extended. Sugges(cid:415)on: Allow both one- and two-part coa(cid:415)ngs. | Column 2 | Column 3 |

pages.text:
• “Specific Gravity – 1.10 to 1.25”
CharCoat IC is formulated with three different solvents, with different evapora(cid:415)on rates, for ease of applica(cid:415)on and
to automa(cid:415)cally yield a naturally uniform, smooth layer. This results in an “as supplied” SG of 1.0. Because the
solvents evaporate, the amount has no effect on the subsequent performance of the coa(cid:415)ng and the SG is thus not
a cri(cid:415)cal parameter.
Sugges(cid:415)on: If it is necessary to specify, allow coa(cid:415)ngs to have an SG from, 0.9 to 1.25.
• “Skin-over (cid:415)me – 15 minutes”
To have a skin-over (cid:415)me of 10 or 20 minutes should not cause any problem. To have to specifically formulate a coat-
ing to have a skin-over (cid:415)me of exactly 15 minutes at 25ºC ambient temperature and 50% rela(cid:415)ve humidity seems an
unnecessary restric(cid:415)on.
Sugges(cid:415)on: Allow a skin-over (cid:415)me of, 10 to 20 minutes at the standard condi(cid:415)ons.
• “Percent Solids Contents, by weight - ≥ 70”
The percentage solids by weight is a somewhat meaningless figure – it is dictated by the quan(cid:415)ty of solvent present
in the material. As the solvent evaporates, 100% solids are le(cid:332) on the insulator. It thus has absolutely no effect on the
performance of the coa(cid:415)ng. Moreover, as Clause 4.4.6 states that “thinning/dilu(cid:415)on shall be required” during appli-
ca(cid:415)on, then the percentage solids will be reduced from the “as supplied” value.
A far more important parameter is the ra(cid:415)o filler and other addi(cid:415)ves to the silicone content. The tendency is for man-
ufacturers to include too much filler because it is significantly less expensive than the silicone and it improves the
results of ar(cid:415)ficial tracking and erosion tests which inten(cid:415)onally destroy the hydrophobicity of the coa(cid:415)ng. Obvious-
ly, under service condi(cid:415)ons, the longer hydrophobicity recovery and transfer (cid:415)mes caused by lower silicone content,
adversely affect the coa(cid:415)ng performance. In our opinion, the ra(cid:415)o of silicone rubber to other solids content should
be at least 55:45, and ideally 60:40.
Sugges(cid:415)on: Rather specify a minimum silicone content of, say, 55% than a minimum solids content which just
reduces the amount of solvent that can be used and necessitates the addi(cid:415)on of more solvent during applica(cid:415)on.
• “Dynamic Viscosity, cP – 1000 to 3500”
The viscosity of CharCoat IC is 325cP. This is the reason that it is so easy to apply and yields a smooth, even finish
which is superior to other products. When applying more viscous coa(cid:415)ngs, as stated in Clause 4.4.6, addi(cid:415)onal
solvent needs to be added during the course of the process as the material has become too thick. CharCoat IC, with
its lower viscosity, completely eliminates the need to handle addi(cid:415)onal hazardous materials during applica(cid:415)on.
Sugges(cid:415)on: A viscosity range of 200 to 2000cP would be more appropriate.
• Note 1: “The coa(cid:415)ng shall be supplied in White Color unless otherwise specified in the data schedule”
There is no evidence to show that a white coloured coa(cid:415)ng would perform any be(cid:425)er than a grey or blue one.
Sugges(cid:415)on: Delete this note.
Clause 4.3.2, Table 02: RTV Coa(cid:415)ng Proper(cid:415)es as Cured.
• “Dielectric Strength, V/mil (ASTM D149), minimum – 350 to 560”
As a minimum value is specified, a single figure, not a range, should be stated.
Sugges(cid:415)on: Specify a single value of, say, 500V/mil.

This results in an “as supplied” SG of 1.0. Because the solvents evaporate, the amount has no effect on the subsequent performance of the coa(cid:415)ng and the SG is thus not a cri(cid:415)cal parameter. Sugges(cid:415)on: If it is necessary to specify, allow coa(cid:415)ngs to have an SG from, 0.9 to 1.25. • “Skin-over (cid:415)me – 15 minutes” To have a skin-over (cid:415)me of 10 or 20 minutes should not cause any problem. To have to specifically formulate a coat- ing to have a skin-over (cid:415)me of exactly 15 minutes at 25ºC ambient temperature and 50% rela(cid:415)ve humidity seems an unnecessary restric(cid:415)on. Sugges(cid:415)on: Allow a skin-over (cid:415)me of, 10 to 20 minutes at the standard condi(cid:415)ons. • “Percent Solids Contents, by weight - ≥ 70” The percentage solids by weight is a somewhat meaningless figure – it is dictated by the quan(cid:415)ty of solvent present in the material. As the solvent evaporates, 100% solids are le(cid:332) on the insulator. It thus has absolutely no effect on the performance of the coa(cid:415)ng. Moreover, as Clause 4.4.6 states that “thinning/dilu(cid:415)on shall be required” during appli- ca(cid:415)on, then the percentage solids will be reduced from the “as supplied” value. A far more important parameter is the ra(cid:415)o filler and other addi(cid:415)ves to the silicone content. The tendency is for man- ufacturers to include too much filler because it is significantly less expensive than the silicone and it improves the results of ar(cid:415)ficial tracking and erosion tests which inten(cid:415)onally destroy the hydrophobicity of the coa(cid:415)ng. Obvious- ly, under service condi(cid:415)ons, the longer hydrophobicity recovery and transfer (cid:415)mes caused by lower silicone content, adversely affect the coa(cid:415)ng performance. In our opinion, the ra(cid:415)o of silicone rubber to other solids content should be at least 55:45, and ideally 60:40. Sugges(cid:415)on: Rather specify a minimum silicone content of, say, 55% than a minimum solids content which just reduces the amount of solvent that can be used and necessitates the addi(cid:415)on of more solvent during applica(cid:415)on. • “Dynamic Viscosity, cP – 1000 to 3500” The viscosity of CharCoat IC is 325cP. This is the reason that it is so easy to apply and yields a smooth, even finish which is superior to other products. When applying more viscous coa(cid:415)ngs, as stated in Clause 4.4.6, addi(cid:415)onal solvent needs to be added during the course of the process as the material has become too thick. CharCoat IC, with its lower viscosity, completely eliminates the need to handle addi(cid:415)onal hazardous materials during applica(cid:415)on. Sugges(cid:415)on: A viscosity range of 200 to 2000cP would be more appropriate. • Note 1: “The coa(cid:415)ng shall be supplied in White Color unless otherwise specified in the data schedule” There is no evidence to show that a white coloured coa(cid:415)ng would perform any be(cid:425)er than a grey or blue one. Sugges(cid:415)on: Delete this note. Clause 4.3.2, Table 02: RTV Coa(cid:415)ng Proper(cid:415)es as Cured. • “Dielectric Strength, V/mil (ASTM D149), minimum – 350 to 560” As a minimum value is specified, a single figure, not a range, should be stated. Sugges(cid:415)on: Specify a single value of, say, 500V/mil.

Because the solvents evaporate, the amount has no effect on the subsequent performance of the coa(cid:415)ng and the SG is thus not a cri(cid:415)cal parameter. Moreover, as Clause 4.4.6 states that “thinning/dilu(cid:415)on shall be required” during appli- ca(cid:415)on, then the percentage solids will be reduced from the “as supplied” value. A far more important parameter is the ra(cid:415)o filler and other addi(cid:415)ves to the silicone content. The tendency is for man- ufacturers to include too much filler because it is significantly less expensive than the silicone and it improves the results of ar(cid:415)ficial tracking and erosion tests which inten(cid:415)onally destroy the hydrophobicity of the coa(cid:415)ng. In our opinion, the ra(cid:415)o of silicone rubber to other solids content should be at least 55:45, and ideally 60:40. pages.text:
• “Volume Resis(cid:415)vity, Ohm.cm (ASTM D257) – 1x1014 to 37x1014”
Only a minimum value should be specified – there should not be an upper limit
Sugges(cid:415)on: Just specify, say, 1x1014 ohm.cm minimum.
• “Tracking Wheel Withstand, hours, minimum – 1000”
The test is undefined. Details of the procedure are required in order to perform the test. Parameters such as we(cid:427)ng
(cid:415)mes, drying (cid:415)mes, (cid:415)me allowed for hydrophobicity recovery, etc., are cri(cid:415)cal to the outcome.
It is not possible to accurately simulate and, in par(cid:415)cular, accelerate service condi(cid:415)ons in a laboratory test. Silicone
rubber, with its (cid:415)me-dependent hydrophobicity transfer and recovery characteris(cid:415)cs, makes the undertaking of a
meaningful ar(cid:415)ficial test even more difficult. For this reason, evalua(cid:415)ons of material performance and life are far
be(cid:425)er performed in a severe, but natural, environment.
Sugges(cid:415)on: If such a test is to be included in the specifica(cid:415)on, it needs to be fully defined and its relevance to
in-service performance confirmed.Tes(cid:415)ng under natural environmental condi(cid:415)ons should be permi(cid:425)ed as an alter-
na(cid:415)ve.
• “Dry Arc Resistance, Track, Seconds – 180 to 200”
As men(cid:415)oned above, the test procedure needs to be defined. Further, only a minimum value should be given – there
should not be an upper limit.
Sugges(cid:415)on: If such a test is to be included in the specifica(cid:415)on, it needs to be fully defined. Only a minimum value
should be stated. Tes(cid:415)ng under natural environmental condi(cid:415)ons should be permi(cid:425)ed as an alterna(cid:415)ve.
Clause 4.4.4.
It is not clear why the coa(cid:415)ng hardness should be specified. However, in general terms, the more elas(cid:415)c the coa(cid:415)ng
is, the be(cid:425)er. For example, wind borne sand will cause more abrasion of hard, rigid surfaces than flexible ones.
Sugges(cid:415)on: If it is considered that the hardness of the material needs to be specified, it should be a maximum, not
a minimum, value.
Clause 4.4.5.
CharCoat IC can be applied to any desired thickness but we consider 0,25 to 0,35mm to be the op(cid:415)mum range. Ther-
mal transfer between the coa(cid:415)ng surface and the ceramic substrate should not be inhibited by too thick a layer.
The issue of coa(cid:415)ng thickness is addressed in Clause 6.6.2. of IEEE Standard 1523, which states:
“The normal thickness suggested by several manufacturers has been 0.5mm. This thickness is a prac(cid:415)cal “rule of
thumb guide” that has been used for years in the coa(cid:415)ng industry and as such has li(cid:425)le significance to coa(cid:415)ng of elec-
trical insulators. The experience to date suggests that coa(cid:415)ng thickness is not a factor in either the performance or
the life of the coa(cid:415)ng. Coa(cid:415)ng thicknesses in the range of 0.125 to 0.7mm have been applied in the field with equal
success”. “Laboratory tests indicate that when leakage current of a damaging magnitude develops on coa(cid:415)ngs, then
thickness plays a role in the coa(cid:415)ng life. Thick coa(cid:415)ngs provide increased thermal resistance to heat generated by dry
band arcing and as such do not allow the heat to be conducted away to the porcelain substrate as quickly as thinner
coa(cid:415)ngs. Thick coa(cid:415)ngs can result in higher spot temperatures during dry band arcing, thereby causing thermal
degrada(cid:415)on of the coa(cid:415)ng sooner than thinner coa(cid:415)ngs”.

rows: • “Volume Resis(cid:415)vity, Ohm.cm (ASTM D257) – 1x1014 to 37x1014” Only a minimum value should be specified – there should not be an upper limit Sugges(cid:415)on: Just specify, say, 1x1014 ohm.cm minimum. • “Tracking Wheel Withstand, hours, minimum – 1000” The test is undefined. Details of the procedure are required in order to perform the test. Parameters such as we(cid:427)ng (cid:415)mes, drying (cid:415)mes, (cid:415)me allowed for hydrophobicity recovery, etc., are cri(cid:415)cal to the outcome. It is not possible to accurately simulate and, in par(cid:415)cular, accelerate service condi(cid:415)ons in a laboratory test. Silicone rubber, with its (cid:415)me-dependent hydrophobicity transfer and recovery characteris(cid:415)cs, makes the undertaking of a meaningful ar(cid:415)ficial test even more difficult. For this reason, evalua(cid:415)ons of material performance and life are far be(cid:425)er performed in a severe, but natural, environment. Sugges(cid:415)on: If such a test is to be included in the specifica(cid:415)on, it needs to be fully defined and its relevance to in-service performance confirmed.Tes(cid:415)ng under natural environmental condi(cid:415)ons should be permi(cid:425)ed as an alter- na(cid:415)ve. • “Dry Arc Resistance, Track, Seconds – 180 to 200” As men(cid:415)oned above, the test procedure needs to be defined. Further, only a minimum value should be given – there should not be an upper limit. Sugges(cid:415)on: If such a test is to be included in the specifica(cid:415)on, it needs to be fully defined. Only a minimum value should be stated. Tes(cid:415)ng under natural environmental condi(cid:415)ons should be permi(cid:425)ed as an alterna(cid:415)ve. Clause 4.4.4. It is not clear why the coa(cid:415)ng hardness should be specified. However, in general terms, the more elas(cid:415)c the coa(cid:415)ng is, the be(cid:425)er. For example, wind borne sand will cause more abrasion of hard, rigid surfaces than flexible ones. Sugges(cid:415)on: If it is considered that the hardness of the material needs to be specified, it should be a maximum, not a minimum, value. Clause 4.4.5. CharCoat IC can be applied to any desired thickness but we consider 0,25 to 0,35mm to be the op(cid:415)mum range. Ther- mal transfer between the coa(cid:415)ng surface and the ceramic substrate should not be inhibited by too thick a layer. The issue of coa(cid:415)ng thickness is addressed in Clause 6.6.2. of IEEE Standard 1523, which states: “The normal thickness suggested by several manufacturers has been 0.5mm. This thickness is a prac(cid:415)cal “rule of thumb guide” that has been used for years in the coa(cid:415)ng industry and as such has li(cid:425)le significance to coa(cid:415)ng of elec- trical insulators. The experience to date suggests that coa(cid:415)ng thickness is not a factor in either the performance or the life of the coa(cid:415)ng. Coa(cid:415)ng thicknesses in the range of 0.125 to 0.7mm have been applied in the field with equal success”. “Laboratory tests indicate that when leakage current of a damaging magnitude develops on coa(cid:415)ngs, then thickness plays a role in the coa(cid:415)ng life. Thick coa(cid:415)ngs provide increased thermal resistance to heat generated by dry band arcing and as such do not allow the heat to be conducted away to the porcelain substrate as quickly as thinner coa(cid:415)ngs. Thick coa(cid:415)ngs can result in higher spot temperatures during dry band arcing, thereby causing thermal degrada(cid:415)on of the coa(cid:415)ng sooner than thinner coa(cid:415)ngs”.

pages.text:
The diagrams below show the insulator temperature change when coated and uncoated porcelain insulators are
externally heated and cooled. This illustrates the high thermal resistance of silicone rubber and why thermal effects
need to be considered.
Clause 4.4.6
As men(cid:415)oned under Clause 4.3.1., Mace Technologies selected a two-part RTV silicone rubber for the CharCoat IC
product. This means that a pre-measured small bo(cid:425)le of Catalyst is supplied with each container of CharCoat IC. The
catalyst is added to the 1 litre or 5 litre can of coa(cid:415)ng which is then shaken prior to use. This is a quick and simple
opera(cid:415)on and offers the following advantages:
o The curing of the rubber is more controlled and not dictated by the amount of moisture in the air.
o Because an outside agent is required for the rubber to cure, the shelf life of the material is considerably
extended.
The requirement that “thinning/dilu(cid:415)on shall be required” and that the manufacturer must “supply the suitable thin-
ner and submit a table showing the percentage quan(cid:415)ty to be used” is astounding. Why this need for on-site hazard-
ous material handling is condoned, whilst the addi(cid:415)on of a small amount (180mm per 5 litres) of inert catalyst before
spraying is forbidden, is incomprehensible. CharCoat IC requires no extra solvent addi(cid:415)on and is thus safer in terms
of both health and fire risks.
Sugges(cid:415)on: Allow both one- and two-part coa(cid:415)ngs. The need for the addi(cid:415)on of hazardous materials during the
applica(cid:415)on process should not “be required” but rather be forbidden.
Clause 4.4.8
Silicone rubber does not naturally adhere to other materials – hence its widespread use in the manufacture of
moulds. Thus, if the applica(cid:415)on of a primer is prohibited, then a bonding agent has to be included in the coa(cid:415)ng itself.
This means that the percentage of silicone in the coa(cid:415)ng is reduced and a significant volume of bonding agent is
dispersed within the layer where it serves no beneficial purpose. With the separate applica(cid:415)on of a primer, the bond-
ing agent exists only where it is required, i.e. at the interface between the coa(cid:415)ng and the porcelain, and no extrane-
ous material is displacing the silicone content.
As illustrated below, a(cid:332)er a 12 cycle,100 hour boiling test, the specimen with a pre-applied primer achieves far be(cid:425)er
adhesion than when 10% by weight of bonding agent is added to the coa(cid:415)ng or when no primer or bonding agent is
used.
Sugges(cid:415)on: The use of a primer yields a higher silicone content coa(cid:415)ng and superior adhesion and thus should be
permi(cid:425)ed.

pages.text:
Clause 4.4.9.
As described above, excellent adhesion of silicone rubber to a ceramic substrate without a bonding agent contained
within the material or applied as a primer, is not possible.
Sugges(cid:415)on: Remove the requirement “excellent unprimed adhesion”.
Clause 4.4.10
For ease of use and to minimize wastage on site, CharCoat IC is supplied in 1 litre and 5 litre containers.
Sugges(cid:415)on: The supply of coa(cid:415)ng in containers of less than 20kg capacity should be permi(cid:425)ed.
Clause 4.5.7
As men(cid:415)oned for Clauses 4.3.1 and 4.4.6, we prefer a two-part silicone rubber to a one-part.
The evapora(cid:415)on rates of non-flammable solvents are generally too low for the easy and uniform spray applica(cid:415)on of
silicone rubber coa(cid:415)ng. Further, the skin-over and tack-free (cid:415)mes are increased. As most coa(cid:415)ng applica(cid:415)ons are
undertaken under non-energized condi(cid:415)ons, the flammability of the coa(cid:415)ng is not usually of concern.
Sugges(cid:415)on: Two-part coa(cid:415)ngs should be permi(cid:425)ed. For be(cid:425)er surface smoothness, uniformity and coverage, flam-
mable solvents should be used. Non-flammable solvents should only be specified for specific live-line applica(cid:415)ons.
Clause 6.0
The values on the Data Schedule should be modified to be compa(cid:415)ble with any changes made to the main body of
the document.
GENERAL COMMENTS
The SEC Specifica(cid:415)on is wri(cid:425)en for the kind of coa(cid:415)ngs tradi(cid:415)onally used by the u(cid:415)lity. CharCoat IC, however, repre-
sen(cid:415)ng a more modern philosophy to the insulator pollu(cid:415)on problem and thus of different formula(cid:415)on, is conse-
quently not covered by the standard. Its effec(cid:415)veness and superiority over other materials is, though, well proven.
This is evident from Figure 2 which provides the result of coa(cid:415)ng evalua(cid:415)on tests at the severe natural test sta(cid:415)on at
Eskom’s Koeberg facility on the west coast of South Africa.
Figure 2: Accumulated
coulomb ampere
leakage currents meas-
ured on coated and
uncoated porcelain
insulators over a period
of one year.

Non-flammable solvents should only be specified for specific live-line applica(cid:415)ons. Clause 6.0 The values on the Data Schedule should be modified to be compa(cid:415)ble with any changes made to the main body of the document. GENERAL COMMENTS The SEC Specifica(cid:415)on is wri(cid:425)en for the kind of coa(cid:415)ngs tradi(cid:415)onally used by the u(cid:415)lity. CharCoat IC, however, repre- sen(cid:415)ng a more modern philosophy to the insulator pollu(cid:415)on problem and thus of different formula(cid:415)on, is conse- quently not covered by the standard. Its effec(cid:415)veness and superiority over other materials is, though, well proven. This is evident from Figure 2 which provides the result of coa(cid:415)ng evalua(cid:415)on tests at the severe natural test sta(cid:415)on at Eskom’s Koeberg facility on the west coast of South Africa. Figure 2: Accumulated coulomb ampere leakage currents meas- ured on coated and uncoated porcelain insulators over a period of one year. | Column 2 | Column 3 |
| --- | --- | --- |
|  |  |  |

images.context_text: Clause 4.4.9. As described above, excellent adhesion of silicone rubber to a ceramic substrate without a bonding agent contained within the material or applied as a primer, is not possible. Sugges(cid:415)on: Remove the requirement “excellent unprimed adhesion”. Clause 4.4.10 For ease of use and to minimize wastage on site, CharCoat IC is supplied in 1 litre and 5 litre containers. Sugges(cid:415)on: The supply of coa(cid:415)ng in containers of less than 20kg capacity should be permi(cid:425)ed. Clause 4.5.7 As men(cid:415)oned for Clauses 4.3.1 and 4.4.6, we prefer a two-part silicone rubber to a one-part. The evapora(cid:415)on rates of non-flammable solvents are generally too low for the easy and uniform spray applica(cid:415)on of silicone rubber coa(cid:415)ng. Further, the skin-over and tack-free (cid:415)mes are increased. As most coa(cid:415)ng applica(cid:415)ons are undertaken under non-energized condi(cid:415)ons, the flammability of the coa(cid:415)ng is not usually of concern. Sugges(cid:415)on: Two-part coa(cid:415)ngs should be permi(cid:425)ed. For be(cid:425)er surface smoothness, uniformity and coverage, flam- mable solvents should be used. Non-flammable solvents should only be specified for specific live-line applica(cid:415)ons. Clause 6.0 The values on the Data Schedule should be modified to be compa(cid:415)ble with any changes made to the main body of the document. GENERAL COMMENTS The SEC Specifica(cid:415)on is wri(cid:425)en for the kind of coa(cid:415)ngs tradi(cid:415)onally used by the u(cid:415)lity. CharCoat IC, however, repre- sen(cid:415)ng a more modern philosophy to the insulator pollu(cid:415)on problem and thus of different formula(cid:415)on, is conse- quently not covered by the standard. Its effec(cid:415)veness and superiority over other materials is, though, well proven. This is evident from Figure 2 which provides the result of coa(cid:415)ng evalua(cid:415)on tests at the severe natural test sta(cid:415)on at Eskom’s Koeberg facility on the west coast of South Africa. Figure 2: Accumulated coulomb ampere leakage currents meas- ured on coated and uncoated porcelain insulators over a period of one year.

pages.text:
The graph indicates the accumulated leakage current ac(cid:415)vity over a one year period on three iden(cid:415)cal porcelain insu-
lators – two of which were coated with different coa(cid:415)ngs and one le(cid:332) uncoated. Details of the test procedure and
assessment method are defined in the Eskom Standard 34-224. In order for a product to pass the test and gain
approval for use on the Eskom system, no more than three flashovers are permi(cid:425)ed over the one year test period. As
shown in the Figure, the uncoated porcelain had the highest leakage current ac(cid:415)vity and experienced its fourth flash-
over a(cid:332)er 6 months. Coa(cid:415)ng Type 2 – a material used widely in the Middle East and complying fully with the SEC
Specifica(cid:415)on – failed a(cid:332)er suffering its fourth flashover in the ninth month. CharCoat IC displayed far lower leakage
current amplitudes and had not one flashover in the year. CharCoat IC is the only coa(cid:415)ng to have achieved this
dis(cid:415)nc(cid:415)on. (The Eskom Test Cer(cid:415)ficate is a(cid:425)ached for reference).
Perhaps the main reason why CharCoat IC differs from its counterparts is the fact that it has not been developed by
a polymer materials manufacturer as an addi(cid:415)onal outlet for their exis(cid:415)ng product range but by a company specializ-
ing in the design and supply of outdoor insulators and insula(cid:415)on upgrading solu(cid:415)ons.

| The graph indicates the accumulated leakage current ac(cid:415)vity over a one year period on three iden(cid:415)cal porcelain insu- lators – two of which were coated with different coa(cid:415)ngs and one le(cid:332) uncoated. Details of the test procedure and assessment method are defined in the Eskom Standard 34-224. In order for a product to pass the test and gain approval for use on the Eskom system, no more than three flashovers are permi(cid:425)ed over the one year test period. As shown in the Figure, the uncoated porcelain had the highest leakage current ac(cid:415)vity and experienced its fourth flash- over a(cid:332)er 6 months. Coa(cid:415)ng Type 2 – a material used widely in the Middle East and complying fully with the SEC Specifica(cid:415)on – failed a(cid:332)er suffering its fourth flashover in the ninth month. CharCoat IC displayed far lower leakage current amplitudes and had not one flashover in the year. CharCoat IC is the only coa(cid:415)ng to have achieved this dis(cid:415)nc(cid:415)on. (The Eskom Test Cer(cid:415)ficate is a(cid:425)ached for reference).
